title: Macroscopic entanglement between two magnon modes via two-tone driving of a superconducting qubit
authors: Rong-Can Yang, Gang Liu, Gen Li, Jie Li
abstract: The cavity-mediated coupling between magnons in an yttrium-iron-garnet (YIG) sphere and a superconducting qubit has recently been demonstrated as a new platform for preparing macroscopic quantum states. Here, based on this system, we propose to entangle two magnon modes in two YIG spheres by driving the qubit with a two-tone field and by appropriately choosing the frequencies and strengths of the two driving fields. We show that strong entanglement can be achieved with fully feasible parameters. We further provide a detection scheme for experimentally verifying the entanglement. Our results indicate that macroscopic entanglement between two magnon modes in two millimeter-sized YIG spheres, involving more than $10^{18}$ spins, can be realized using currently available parameters, which finds promising applications in fundamental studies, such as macroscopic quantum mechanics and the test of unconventional decoherence theories.
summary: The paper proposes a method to entangle two massive magnon modes in separate YIG spheres using a superconducting qubit driven by two microwave tones. It shows that this protocol can create entanglement involving a huge number of spins, offering a way to study macroscopic quantum phenomena.
main_problem: The paper aims to propose a protocol for generating and detecting macroscopic entanglement between two magnon modes in separate YIG spheres.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that a two-tone driving of a superconducting qubit can induce a two-mode squeezing interaction, achieving significant entanglement involving over 10^18 spins.
method: The study uses the James-Jerke formula and adiabatic elimination of the cavity mode to derive an effective Hamiltonian, then solves the Lindblad master equation to simulate entanglement dynamics and Wigner function tomography.
model_or_system: A hybrid cavity-magnon-qubit system consisting of two millimeter-sized YIG spheres and one transmon-type superconducting qubit, all placed within a common 3D microwave cavity.
key_observables: Logarithmic Negativity (LN), joint Wigner functions, and joint magnon-number parity.
important_parameters: Magnon frequencies around 5.8 GHz, qubit frequency around 5.8 GHz, coupling strengths in the MHz range, and temperatures below 100 mK.
main_assumptions: Large detuning of the cavity mode from the qubit and magnons (adiabatic elimination), and the validity of the Rotating-Wave Approximation (RWA).
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the system schematic; Fig 2 compares entanglement evolution between effective and original Hamiltonians; Fig 3 shows entanglement sensitivity to dissipation; Fig 4 shows temperature effects; Fig 5 displays joint Wigner functions for low and high decay rates.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the system and problem, derives the effective Hamiltonian via two-tone driving and adiabatic elimination, performs numerical simulations of entanglement dynamics and noise sensitivity, proposes a detection scheme via Wigner function tomography, and concludes with the implications for macroscopic quantum mechanics.
why_interesting: It provides a theoretical framework for generating macroscopic entanglement in a hybrid platform, which is highly relevant for studying the quantum-to-classical transition and testing unconventional decoherence theories in open quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) The cavity–magnon–qubit system used for generat- ing macroscopic entanglement between two magnon modes. Two magnon modes in two YIG spheres and a transmon superconduct- ing qubit couple to a common microwave cavity mode via magnetic- and electric-dipole interactions, respectively. The two YIG spheres are placed inside a uniform bias magnetic field B0, and each sphere is further in a local magnetic field provided by a small coil (not shown). The qubit is driven by two microwave fields. (b) Original and effec- tive frequencies of the system with two qubit drive frequencies. The cavity mode at frequency ωc is far-detuned from both the magnon modes at ωm1 and ωm2 and the qubit at...

FIG. 2. Entanglement between two magnon modes as a function of time. The black solid curve is obtained from the derived effective Hamiltonian (9), while the other three curves are achieved from the original Hamiltonian (3) for three values of Ω1/2π = 100, 120, and 150 MHz. Other parameters are given in the main text.

FIG. 3. Maximum magnon entanglement as a function of (a) the qubit dissipation rate γd and dephasing rate γϕ at a fixed magnon decay rate κ1,2/2π = 0.5 MHz; and (b) the magnon and qubit dissi- pation rates κ ≡κ1,2 and γ ≡γd,ϕ. The other parameters are those as in Fig. 2.
FIG. 4. Magnon entanglement as a function of time for different bath temperatures. The parameters are identical to those in Fig. 2.

FIG. 5. Joint Wigner function W(X1, X2) in (a) and (c) and W(P1, P2) in (b) and (d) of two magnon modes at the optimal time giving the maximum entanglement. In (a)-(b), κ/2π = 0.5 MHz, and (c)-(d) κ/2π = 2 MHz. The other parameters are identical to those in Fig. 2.

Spintronics.note: The paper directly addresses the interface of quantum optics and spintronics by using a superconducting qubit to induce entanglement between magnon modes in a hybrid cavity-magnon system.
